limitation: This is a heuristic scanner for agent review triage, not a security or performance oracle. It can miss vulnerabilities and bottlenecks and produce false positives; verify findings manually against current WordPress docs, profiling, and project context.

findings.message: REST route uses __return_true as permission_callback.
findings.remediation: Confirm the endpoint is intentionally public and does not expose private data or mutation operations.

findings.message: Dynamic block render output should be validated and escaped.
findings.remediation: Treat attributes as untrusted in render.php or render callbacks; validate, sanitize, authorize if needed, and escape late.

findings.message: flush_rewrite_rules() appears outside activation/deactivation/uninstall context.
findings.why: Flushing rewrite rules is expensive and writes rewrite state; it should not run on normal requests.
findings.remediation: Move rewrite flushing to activation/deactivation or a versioned migration after rewrite-dependent objects are registered.

findings.message: admin_enqueue_scripts callback has no obvious screen check.
findings.why: Admin assets loaded on every wp-admin screen slow unrelated admin workflows.
findings.remediation: Gate by $hook_suffix or get_current_screen()->id before enqueueing plugin-specific assets.

findings.message: Script enqueue has no obvious version argument.
findings.why: Missing versions can make cache invalidation unreliable for static assets.
findings.remediation: Use plugin version or generated .asset.php metadata for dependencies and versioning.

findings.message: Script enqueue does not show an explicit footer/strategy decision.
findings.why: Non-critical frontend scripts can often avoid blocking render when loaded in the footer or with a safe loading strategy.
findings.remediation: Verify target WordPress support and add a footer or loading strategy only when dependencies and behavior remain correct.

findings.message: Query has no obvious no_found_rows setting.
findings.why: When pagination totals are not needed, avoiding FOUND_ROWS-style totals can reduce database work.
findings.remediation: Set no_found_rows => true for non-paginated queries where total counts are not needed.

findings.message: Query requests all matching posts with -1.
findings.why: Unbounded post queries can load large datasets and exhaust memory on production sites.
findings.remediation: Add pagination or a safe upper bound. Use fields => ids when only IDs are needed.

findings.message: Query has no obvious no_found_rows setting.
findings.why: When pagination totals are not needed, avoiding FOUND_ROWS-style totals can reduce database work.
findings.remediation: Set no_found_rows => true for non-paginated queries where total counts are not needed.

findings.message: Query requests all matching posts with -1.
findings.why: Unbounded post queries can load large datasets and exhaust memory on production sites.
findings.remediation: Add pagination or a safe upper bound. Use fields => ids when only IDs are needed.

findings.message: Query has no obvious no_found_rows setting.
findings.why: When pagination totals are not needed, avoiding FOUND_ROWS-style totals can reduce database work.
findings.remediation: Set no_found_rows => true for non-paginated queries where total counts are not needed.

findings.message: WP_Query/get_posts appears inside a loop.
findings.why: Queries inside loops often create N+1 query patterns.
findings.remediation: Prefetch data, batch IDs, prime caches, or move the query outside the loop.

findings.message: Direct SQL SELECT has no obvious LIMIT.
findings.why: Unbounded SQL can scan and return too many rows on production data.
findings.remediation: Add LIMIT/OFFSET or use a paginated WordPress API. Keep using $wpdb->prepare() for variables.

findings.message: set_transient() appears without an expiration argument.
findings.why: Non-expiring transients can persist indefinitely and may be autoloaded when stored in options.
findings.remediation: Add an explicit TTL and an invalidation strategy.

findings.message: Transient writes found with no delete_transient() in the same file.
findings.why: Caches that depend on content/settings can serve stale output without invalidation hooks.
findings.remediation: Add invalidation on relevant content, option, term, or plugin-specific change hooks, or document TTL-only behavior.

findings.message: REST route has no obvious schema or response shape control nearby.
findings.why: Small response shapes reduce payload size and client-side work.
findings.remediation: Return only required fields and add schema/field controls where practical.

findings.message: Dynamic block render code runs a query without obvious caching.
findings.why: Dynamic block render callbacks can run on every page view where the block appears.
findings.remediation: Bound the query and add fragment caching with invalidation when output is safe to cache.

findings.message: block.json declares frontend-capable script assets.
findings.why: Frontend block scripts add bytes and runtime work to pages where the block appears.
findings.remediation: Verify the script is needed on the frontend and keep editor/view scripts split.

findings.message: Cron/background code appears to run an unbounded post query.
findings.why: Long cron runs can overlap, time out, or block other scheduled work.
findings.remediation: Batch work with limits, offsets/cursors, locks, and idempotent processing.

findings.message: Expensive cron/background work has no obvious lock.
findings.why: Without a lock, overlapping runs can duplicate work or increase load.
findings.remediation: Use a short-lived transient/object-cache lock and make the job idempotent.

performance.limitation: Performance findings are static heuristics. They can miss bottlenecks and produce false positives; verify with profiling, production-sized data, and current WordPress docs.
